metadata.title: VaR and CVaR — Risk Metrics from Return Distributions with Claude
metadata.note: CLI video spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

After the 2008 financial crisis, regulators asked banks a simple question: how much can you lose on a bad day? Value at Risk — VaR — became the standard answer. The 95 percent one-day VaR is the loss level such that only 5 percent of days are worse. But VaR has a flaw: it says nothing about what happens in those 5 percent of days. CVaR — Conditional Value at Risk — answers that question: the expected loss given that you are already in the tail.
beats.visual_intent: title card: return distribution, VaR threshold, tail region highlighted

beats.narration_text: Type the prompt. Generate 10,000 daily returns from GBM, mu 3 basis points per day, sigma 1 percent per day. Compute historical VaR at 95 and 99 percent, parametric VaR under the normal assumption, CVaR at both levels. Print a two-row comparison table.

props.command:
claude "Write risk_metrics.py: generate 10,000 daily returns from GBM
(mu=0.0003/day, sigma=0.01/day). Compute historical VaR at
95%%/99%%, parametric VaR assuming normality, CVaR at both
levels. Print 2-row comparison table: Historical vs Parametric
for VaR95, VaR99, CVaR95, CVaR99."

beats.narration_text: Read it. Historical VaR at 95 percent is just the negative 5th percentile of the return distribution — no model, just the data. Parametric VaR assumes normality: negative mu plus 1.645 sigma. CVaR is the conditional mean: average all returns below the VaR threshold. The difference between the two methods exposes fat tails.

props.code:
# risk_metrics.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(123)
returns = rng.normal(0.0003, 0.01, 10_000)

# Historical VaR (no model assumption)
var_95 = -np.percentile(returns, 5)   # 95%% VaR
var_99 = -np.percentile(returns, 1)   # 99%% VaR

# CVaR (expected loss in the tail)
cvar_95 = -returns[returns < -var_95].mean()
cvar_99 = -returns[returns < -var_99].mean()

beats.narration_text: Run it. The histogram shows the return distribution — nearly normal, centered near zero. The crimson bars are the 5 percent left tail — the worst days. The first dashed line is the 95 percent VaR threshold; the second, darker line is the 99 percent VaR. CVaR is the expected value of the returns to the left of each threshold — the average bad day, given that it is a bad day.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: return histogram with tail bars in crimson, VaR95 and VaR99 dashed lines, tail region labeled

beats.narration_text: What if the returns are fat-tailed? Historical VaR from a normal distribution is too optimistic. Ask Claude to replace the normal with a Student t-distribution with 4 degrees of freedom and recompute the comparison table.

props.command:
claude "update risk_metrics.py:
  -> replace normal distribution with Student t (df=4)
  -> recompute historical and parametric VaR/CVaR
  -> show how fat tails inflate the 99%% CVaR"

beats.narration_text: With t-distributed returns, the 99 percent CVaR is roughly 60 percent larger than the normal estimate. The 95 percent VaR is similar — but the 99 percent CVaR tells the real story: on the worst 1 percent of days, the average loss is nearly twice what a Gaussian model predicts. That gap was the model risk in 2008.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: VaR/CVaR comparison table, normal vs fat-tailed; gap at 99% CVaR highlighted

beats.narration_text: Your move: replace simulated returns with real historical data. Pull daily S&P 500 returns from yfinance and compute VaR and CVaR historically. Compare the 2008 realized losses to the model prediction from 2007 data.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: real S&P 500 returns, 2008 crisis tail, model vs realized

beats.narration_text: Then compute portfolio VaR with correlated assets. Multivariate normal VaR has a closed form; multivariate t requires Monte Carlo. The correlation structure matters — crisis correlations spike, and that is what breaks the normal VaR model.
beats.visual_intent: code snippet: multivariate normal VaR with Cholesky; note on correlation spike

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
